caption:
get my full Tokyo travel guide + itinerary at the link in my bio 🇯🇵

we let our stomachs guide us around Japan, and after eating enough to sustain 5 grown men for a whole year, here are our top 6 meals we had during our trip!!

#japanfood #japanestaurants #tokyofood #kyotofood #japantravel

Menchirashi Udon | Gykuatsu Motomura |
Koisus Curry I Wagyu Hokusai | Itsuki Chaya Arashiyama | Koyasan | Mount Koya | Kyoto
Food | Tokyo Food I Japan Food | best restaurants in Japan | where to eat in Japan |
Japan trip Itokyo trip - must try food in Japan | tokyo restaurants | kyoto restaurants
less
transcript: We flew to Japan to do one thing, and that was eat. So here were the top six meals that we had in Japan as two bottomless pits who pretty much planned their trips around food. We're starting off with this incredible Japanese curry dish from Koisis Curry in Kyoto. Our meal was presented like baby Jesus in his manger. Take the tap off the old manger, and there he lay, ready to alter the course of our lives just as this curry did. The dish comes with two different types of curry, plus a pot of dashi to pour over. And no good nativity scene is complete without shrimp tempura. It was served with this amazing matcha salt, which was the absolute cherry on top. Next up, oh my god, the viral carbonara udon from Menchirashi. Whatever genius decided to mix carbonara and udon deserves a Nobel Prize. We also ordered a bunch of the different side add-ons, because go big or go home. The flavor is seriously out of this world, and we did wait 45 minutes to get sat, but I would do it again. Next up was this melt-in-your-mouth wagyu beef cutlet from Gukatsu Motomura. I cannot even tell you the things that I would do to be able to experience this meal again for the first time. They load you up with all sorts of different side dishes and sauces, so you can have a true DIY moment on the grill. Now this was another place where we had to wait 45 minutes in line to get sat, but just view it as 45 extra minutes to get stoked for your cutlet. Next was one of the most beautiful riverside restaurants I've ever been to, called Itsuki Kaya and Arashiyama. This was right outside of Kyoto, near some famous temples and the bamboo forest. I kind of expected this to be one of those places where the food looks really pretty, but has no taste. But alas, they shocked me. Honestly, the most perfect lunch stop we could have had after a day of sightseeing. And next up, man oh man, here's a meal that made me want to cancel my flight back to the States. This is wagyu hokusai in Kyoto. When I die, please bury me in one of their beef boxes. There's just something about the quality of meat they use here that just makes the entire dish insane. And last but not least, I am no vegan, but this vegan set was one of the best dinners I've ever had in my entire life. We did an overnight stay at a Buddhist temple in Koyasan, and this was the dinner we were serving. Every little dish was so unique and flavorful. We even tried to force ourselves to eat super slow so we could have longer to enjoy it. And that's my top six.
